<!DOCTYPE PubmedArticleSet PUBLIC "-//NLM//DTD PubMedArticle, 1st January 2025//EN" "https://dtd.nlm.nih.gov/ncbi/pubmed/out/pubmed_250101.dtd">
<PubmedArticleSet>
<PubmedArticle><MedlineCitation Status="Publisher" Owner="NLM"><PMID Version="1">42440276</PMID><DateRevised><Year>2026</Year><Month>07</Month><Day>13</Day></DateRevised><Article PubModel="Print-Electronic"><Journal><ISSN IssnType="Electronic">1753-4887</ISSN><JournalIssue CitedMedium="Internet"><PubDate><Year>2026</Year><Month>Jul</Month><Day>13</Day></PubDate></JournalIssue><Title>Nutrition reviews</Title><ISOAbbreviation>Nutr Rev</ISOAbbreviation></Journal><ArticleTitle>Duration-Dependent Changes in Body Composition During Prolonged Fasting: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.</ArticleTitle><ELocationID EIdType="pii" ValidYN="Y">nuag092</ELocationID><ELocationID EIdType="doi" ValidYN="Y">10.1093/nutrit/nuag092</ELocationID><Abstract><AbstractText Label="CONTEXT" NlmCategory="BACKGROUND">Prolonged fasting has gained increasing attention as a non-pharmacological strategy for weight reduction and metabolic health improvement. However, the magnitude and composition of fasting-induced weight loss remain unclear, particularly regarding the relative contributions of fat mass, fat-free mass, and total body water, as well as the role of fasting duration.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="OBJECTIVE" NlmCategory="OBJECTIVE">A systematic review and meta-analysis was conducted to quantify the effects of prolonged fasting on body composition outcomes.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="METHODS" NlmCategory="METHODS">The PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus databases were systematically searched for eligible studies reporting pre-post changes in body composition. Standardized mean changes were calculated as Hedges' g and pooled using random-effects models. Duration effects were evaluated using subgroup analyses (≤3 days vs >3 days) and meta-regression with fasting duration as a continuous moderator. Subgroup analyses were also conducted according to baseline weight status (obese vs. healthy/normal weight) and body composition assessment method.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="RESULTS" NlmCategory="RESULTS">A total of 49 studies met the inclusion criteria, contributing 150 independent effect sizes across eligible outcomes. Prolonged fasting produced significant reductions in body weight (g = -0.38) and BMI (g = -0.42). Decreases were also observed in fat mass (g = -0.25), fat-free mass (g = -0.51), total body water (g = -0.68), and waist circumference (g = -0.55). Subgroup analyses indicated that body weight reductions were greater in obese participants than in healthy individuals and were more pronounced when fasting duration exceeded 3 days. Meta-regression analyses did not reveal significant duration-response relationships for BMI, fat mass, or fat-free mass, although a non-significant trend toward greater body weight reduction with longer fasting duration was observed.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="CONCLUSIONS" NlmCategory="CONCLUSIONS">Prolonged fasting reduces body weight, BMI, fat mass, and waist circumference but is also associated with substantial losses in fat-free mass and total body water, indicating that fasting-induced weight loss reflects complex changes in body composition rather than isolated fat loss. No clear duration-dependent effects were observed for most outcomes.</AbstractText><CopyrightInformation>© The Author(s) 2026. Published by Oxford University Press on behalf of the International Life Sciences Institute.</CopyrightInformation></Abstract><AuthorList CompleteYN="Y"><Author care</Title><ISOAbbreviation>Diabetes Care</ISOAbbreviation></Journal><ArticleTitle>Sarcopenic Obesity and Risk of Incident Type 2 Diabetes: A Prospective Cohort Study and Landmark Analysis From the UK Biobank.</ArticleTitle><ELocationID EIdType="pii" ValidYN="Y">dc260797</ELocationID><ELocationID EIdType="doi" ValidYN="Y">10.2337/dc26-0797</ELocationID><Abstract><AbstractText Label="OBJECTIVE" NlmCategory="OBJECTIVE">To examine whether sarcopenic obesity (SO) is associated with incident type 2 diabetes (T2D) beyond obesity or sarcopenia alone and whether associations differ by sex, age, and transitions in body composition phenotypes over time.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="RESEARCH DESIGN AND METHODS" NlmCategory="METHODS">We studied 479,607 diabetes-free UK Biobank participants. SO was defined using handgrip strength, skeletal muscle mass-to-weight ratio, and fat mass percentage. Cox models estimated hazard ratios (HRs). A landmark analysis (n = 53,107) examined phenotype transitions.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="RESULTS" NlmCategory="RESULTS">Over a median follow-up of 14.2 years, T2D developed in 32,948 participants. SO conferred the highest risk (HR 3.54 [95% CI 3.34-3.74]), exceeding obesity alone and sarcopenia alone. Transitioning to SO (HR 2.90 [95% CI 2.07-4.07]) and persistent SO (HR 3.07 [95% CI 1.63-5.79]) both elevated risks.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="CONCLUSIONS" NlmCategory="CONCLUSIONS">SO was associated with higher T2D risk than obesity or sarcopenia alone, supporting integrated assessment of muscle health and adiposity in T2D risk stratification.</AbstractText><CopyrightInformation>© 2026 by the American Diabetes Association.</CopyrightInformation></Abstract><AuthorList CompleteYN="Y"><Author disorders drug targets</Title><ISOAbbreviation>Endocr Metab Immune Disord Drug Targets</ISOAbbreviation></Journal><ArticleTitle>Modified Fuzi Lizhong Decoction Alleviates Colonic Lesions and Modulates Gut Microbiota in Diabetic Gastroparesis.</ArticleTitle><ELocationID EIdType="doi" ValidYN="Y">10.2174/0118715303465130260619065650</ELocationID><Abstract><AbstractText Label="INTRODUCTION" NlmCategory="BACKGROUND">Diabetic gastroparesis (DGP) is a debilitating complication of diabetes mellitus. This study investigated the therapeutic effects of Modified Fuzi Lizhong decoction (MFZLZD) on DGP.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="METHODS" NlmCategory="METHODS">A DGP rat model was established through a high-fat diet combined with an irregular feeding schedule. Rats were treated with MFZLZD (5 or 20 g/kg) or mosapride (positive control; 1 mL/100 g) for 7 weeks. Body weight and gastric parameters (gastric emptying, peristalsis, and blood flow) were assessed. Histopathological alterations in gastric and colonic tissues were evaluated via HE staining, while goblet cell density was quantified by AB-PAS staining. The expression of tight junction proteins was analyzed using immunofluorescence. Finally, 16S rRNA sequencing of fecal samples was performed to characterize alterations in the gut microbiota.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="RESULTS" NlmCategory="RESULTS">MFZLZD significantly increased body weight and effectively restored gastric function in DGP rats. Moreover, MFZLZD treatment attenuated gastric and colonic mucosal injury, preserved goblet cell populations, and upregulated tight junction protein expression. Gut microbiota diversity was altered in DGP rats and was partially restored following MFZLZD treatment. Changes in 33 key microbial taxa were observed between groups.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="DISCUSSION" NlmCategory="CONCLUSIONS">MFZLZD improved gastric function and reinforced intestinal barrier integrity in DGP rats. The observed changes in gut microbiota composition suggest a potential association between alterations in the gut microbiota and the therapeutic effects of MFZLZD, although the underlying mechanisms remain unclear and require further investigation.</AbstractText><AbstractText Label="CONCLUSION" NlmCategory="CONCLUSIONS">MFZLZD represents a promising therapeutic strategy for DGP and is associated with improvements in intestinal barrier integrity, gastric function, and gut microbiota composition.</AbstractText><CopyrightInformation>Copyright© Bentham Science Publishers; For any queries, please email at epub@benthamscience.net.</CopyrightInformation></Abstract><AuthorList CompleteYN="Y"><Author
